## [00:00 - 03:01]

In this lesson, I'm going to show you the exact workflow that I used to scale my outreach and collaborate with hundreds or even thousands of nano and micro influences at once. This is important because reaching out and collaborating with influences can be an extremely time consuming process. So I'm going to show you the tools, automations and outsourcing that I use to be able to do this at scale. If you don't do this, you're either not going to be able to scale your nano and micro influencer outreach to the level required for impact, or you're going to waste a whole lot of time doing it. Also, if you're not focused on leveraging the power of nano and micro influences at scale, you're missing out on an additional revenue stream which is accounting for up to 30% of some brand's monthly sales. The reason that we want to work with nano and micro influences at scale is because they have far less reach than a macro influencer overall, so we need to collaborate with more of them to achieve the same reach. So that's the first reason. The second reason is that influencer marketing has a compounding effect. Think about it this way. It takes a certain amount of time for somebody to be exposed to your brand and products before they trust you to perform certain actions, for example, following on social media, signing up to your email list or ultimately purchasing from your brand. The more we work with influencers at scale, the more times that we can get in front of our audience and build a relationship with them. We know that repetition helps to build recognition and memorability, which helps to build front of mind positioning for your brand, and it also builds familiarity, which helps to build trust. We'll talk about this a little more later, but it would make sense that what we actually want amongst our influencers is audience overlap, because then they start to seemingly see you everywhere. This popularity also acts as social proof for your product, which helps to build even more trust. I call this level of omnipresence the everybody's doing it effect, and we'll discuss a particular campaign to create this effect in our lesson on macro influencer collaborations. So back to doing influencer collaborations at scale. Don't just take my word for it. I know that huge Instagram brands like Frank Body, Triangle, Alaya Skin, High Smile and Movement Watches all send out to hundreds of influencers a month and have been doing so for years. This compounding effect cannot be doubted. In fact, Nathan from Founders Girl for an Emily's brand saw their largest month of sales ever once they started hitting the 100 sendouts a month mark, so that seems to be the magic number for most brands. So I want you to target 50 to 100 sendouts a month for the next three months to start seeing the effects for yourself. Of course, there are some brands that sell high ticket items that won't be able to achieve this scale at a profit. In this case, I'd suggest creating a lower ticket item, even if it's not sold on your

## [03:01 - 06:01]

store, that can be used to tell the story or benefits of your brand, like merch for example. So this could be a hat, a t-shirt, a sweater, a beanie, a drink bottle, the list goes on. You can check out some other brands with cool merch like Fluff and Glossier in the resources section. So hopefully I've sold you on this enough to know that it's something that you want to invest in and try even for those three months. I'd also add in not to give up after one week or one month for example if you're not getting the results that you want yet. The compounding effects of influencer marketing take time to build and we don't want to ruin the momentum. So let's get started on my process. A quick note here is that this is for Instagram influencers in particular, but a lot of this process will be able to be used and mapped out for influencers on other platforms as well in the exact same way. So step one is to create a blank new Instagram account. You can call this anything, it really doesn't matter as we'll only be using this to build a list of influencers to reach out to. So for drop bottle, it could be DB1234 for example if that's not taken, but whatever you like. So anybody that this account is following means you're happy to send them out product to post for you. Remember that we're talking about nano and micro influencers here so make sure that you're not following people with over 50,000 followers and it actually makes sure that they fill more so between the 5 to 15,000 follower mark if that's possible. That's just based off past results. These are the influencers we've found most likely to follow through and post on our brand's behalf simply in return for product. So what we want to do now is find some nano and micro influencers to follow from this account. You might have one or two or even 10 in mind and that'd be a great start. So now we want to think back to our previous lesson on identifying the influential and we want to find a few micro influencers as a base point to start to build off. And what I mean by build off is that from this point we can use these influencers as a base to find many more like them by using Instagram's similar to drop down feature. This is a powerful feature as the Instagram algorithm is highly trained at grouping similar accounts together based off different factors like audience overlap between influencers and other attributes like content that they post which we definitely do not have at our disposal. Instagram has hundreds if not thousands of data points on each and every user and it uses these to group these accounts together into associated accounts as well as niches which are basically algorithmic silos that show people the type of content that they want to see based off what they're previously engaged with. I could talk about the Instagram algorithm for days but for now let's just trust that the similar to function to find us more influencers to follow does work. Basically you want to start by building up this list to 100 influencers and ultimately up to around a thousand influencers so that we can start our next stage which is outreach. Remember that we're going to need far more than we'll actually end up sending out to each month as not every influencer is going to get back to us or be interested in our

## [06:01 - 08:30]

brand or our offer. So let's say we've followed a few hundred influencers on this account. Next we want to hire a VA or virtual assistant and you may already have one or you might want to do this in-house just if you do it's going to cost a lot more money. So our VA's are from the Philippines and they are some of the most lovely incredible people and they're very much a part of our team in the same way that my in-house team are as well. So you need to treat them accordingly. You can find VA's through a platform like Freelancer or Upwork which some of you may have used in the past. So we want to hire a VA that has good skills in English, maybe a background in customer service and data entry for example and also learns very quickly. I've attached a job ad template for finding an A player virtual assistant in the resources below. Then what we want to do is get our VA to start working through every influencer that our list building account for example DB1234 is following. They'll be able to sort these followers by the date that they were followed from earliest to latest or vice versa so that they can easily stay on top of it while you're still following influencers as long as they keep track of where they're up to. And they definitely will as they'll be keeping track of all of this in your outreach tracking spreadsheet. They'll reach out in two ways. The first way is through a direct message or DM via the Instagram platform and this will be from your brand's Instagram profile. So at Drop Bottle for example, not from the list building account. In terms of what they write in their outreach message itself, that's going to be up to your brand and I've provided example scripts of some of the things that we say on Drop Bottle's behalf when reaching out as well as some of our frequently asked questions. If our VA doesn't know the answer to a question they'll flag this response in our inbox so that our customer service or social media teams can go through these and answer them. And if they're recurring they can add them to the FAQ section in the document via VA too. So the VA will send the initial DM outreach message and then they'll head across to the influencer's profile that they've just messaged, like three of their posts to get their attention and then comment on the most recent post. I've added a few examples of what we comment here for Drop Bottle but this might be something as basic as we love your feed, we just sent you a message about joining our Drop Bottle team. Then if we haven't heard back from the influencer in the next three days they'll send a follow up message saying something like just bumping this up in your inbox in case you missed it. Remember we'll be tracking these messages in our outreach spreadsheet which I've provided an example for in the resources section below.
